05 Async Patterns — Filing FOIA Requests

← Back to lessons

You use async/await every day in Next.js. But do you know what happens under the hood? Why await doesn't actually block? Why errors sometimes vanish silently? This lesson strips away the abstraction.

The Mental Model: FOIA Requests

A Promise is a FOIA (Freedom of Information Act) request:

  • You file the request (create the Promise). The work begins immediately.
  • You don't wait at the counter. You go back to your desk and keep working on other stories.
  • Eventually, you get a response (resolved) with the documents you asked for.
  • Or you get a rejection letter (rejected) explaining why they won't release the records.
  • You specified what to do with the documents when you filed (.then) and what to do if rejected (.catch).

The key insight: a Promise is a container for a future value. It's not the value itself. When you write const data = await fetch(url), you're saying "pause this function until the FOIA comes back, but let the rest of the newsroom keep working."

Promises From Scratch

A Promise has 3 states:

  • pending — FOIA filed, waiting for response
  • fulfilled — documents received (resolved with a value)
  • rejected — rejection letter received (rejected with a reason)
const foiaRequest = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
  // The executor function runs immediately
  const documentsFound = true;

  if (documentsFound) {
    resolve({ pages: 500, topic: "Campaign Finance" });
  } else {
    reject(new Error("Records sealed by court order"));
  }
});

// .then() handles fulfillment
// .catch() handles rejection
// .finally() runs regardless
foiaRequest
  .then(docs => console.log(`Received ${docs.pages} pages`))
  .catch(err => console.error(`Denied: ${err.message}`))
  .finally(() => console.log("Request complete"));

Promise Chaining

Each .then() returns a NEW Promise. This is what makes chaining work:

fetch("/api/articles")
  .then(response => response.json())    // Returns a Promise<JSON>
  .then(articles => articles[0])         // Returns a Promise<Article>
  .then(article => article.title)        // Returns a Promise<string>
  .then(title => console.log(title))     // Returns a Promise<void>
  .catch(err => console.error(err));     // Catches ANY error in the chain

// If any .then() throws or returns a rejected Promise,
// execution jumps directly to the nearest .catch()

Critical rule: if you return a value from .then(), the next .then() receives that value. If you return a Promise, the next .then() waits for it to resolve. This is called "Promise unwrapping."

async/await: Syntactic Sugar Over Promises

async/await is not a different mechanism. It's a nicer way to write Promise chains:

Promise chain
function getArticle(id) {
  return fetch(`/api/${id}`)
    .then(r => r.json())
    .then(data => data.title)
    .catch(e => "Not found");
}
async/await (identical behavior)
async function getArticle(id) {
  try {
    const r = await fetch(`/api/${id}`);
    const data = await r.json();
    return data.title;
  } catch (e) {
    return "Not found";
  }
}

What async Does

// An async function ALWAYS returns a Promise
async function getValue() {
  return 42;
}

getValue();             // Promise<42> (not 42!)
await getValue();       // 42

// Even if you throw, it becomes a rejected Promise
async function fail() {
  throw new Error("oops");
}

fail();                 // Promise<rejected: Error("oops")>

What await Does

// await pauses the FUNCTION, not the thread
async function fetchData() {
  console.log("A");
  const data = await fetch("/api/data");
  // Everything after `await` is like a .then() callback
  // This function is suspended, but the event loop continues
  console.log("B");
}

fetchData();
console.log("C");

// Output: A, C, B
// "C" runs while fetchData is suspended at the await

await does not block. It suspends only the current async function and yields control back to the event loop. The rest of your program keeps running. This is why await is only allowed inside async functions — normal functions can't be suspended.

Microtasks Revisited

From Lesson 01, you know microtasks run before macrotasks. Here's the precise Promise microtask behavior:

console.log("1");

const p = Promise.resolve();

p.then(() => {
  console.log("2");
  p.then(() => console.log("3"));
});

p.then(() => console.log("4"));

console.log("5");

// Output: 1, 5, 2, 4, 3
// 1, 5: synchronous
// 2: first .then callback
// 4: second .then callback (queued before 3)
// 3: nested .then (queued DURING microtask processing)

Key rule: microtasks queued during microtask processing run in the SAME microtask cycle. The event loop doesn't move to macrotasks until the microtask queue is completely drained. This means an infinite loop of queueMicrotask can starve the macrotask queue.

Promise Combinators

const p1 = fetch("/api/articles");
const p2 = fetch("/api/authors");
const p3 = fetch("/api/topics");

// Promise.all — wait for ALL to fulfill (fail-fast on first rejection)
const [articles, authors, topics] = await Promise.all([p1, p2, p3]);

// Promise.allSettled — wait for ALL to complete (never rejects)
const results = await Promise.allSettled([p1, p2, p3]);
// results: [{ status: "fulfilled", value: ... }, { status: "rejected", reason: ... }, ...]

// Promise.race — first to settle wins (fulfilled OR rejected)
const fastest = await Promise.race([p1, p2, p3]);

// Promise.any — first to FULFILL wins (ignores rejections)
const firstSuccess = await Promise.any([p1, p2, p3]);
// Only rejects if ALL reject (AggregateError)

Parallel fetching in Next.js: When your page needs data from 3 APIs, don't await them sequentially. Use Promise.all to fire them in parallel. This is one of the biggest performance wins you can get in getServerSideProps or Server Components.

Error Handling Patterns

The Unhandled Rejection Problem

// This SILENTLY fails — the rejection is never caught
async function fetchData() {
  const res = await fetch("/api/data");
  const json = await res.json();
  return json;
}

fetchData(); // No .catch(), no try/catch wrapping
// If fetch fails, you get an "unhandled rejection" warning
// In production, the error vanishes

Robust Error Handling

// Pattern 1: try/catch inside async function
async function fetchData() {
  try {
    const res = await fetch("/api/data");
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);
    return await res.json();
  } catch (err) {
    console.error("Fetch failed:", err);
    return null;
  }
}

// Pattern 2: Go-style tuple returns (no try/catch at call site)
async function safeFetch(url) {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(url);
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status}`);
    const data = await res.json();
    return [data, null];
  } catch (err) {
    return [null, err];
  }
}

const [data, error] = await safeFetch("/api/data");
if (error) {
  // Handle error
}

// Pattern 3: Global safety net
window.addEventListener("unhandledrejection", (event) => {
  console.error("Unhandled rejection:", event.reason);
  event.preventDefault();
});

Common async/await Pitfalls

// PITFALL 1: Sequential when you mean parallel
// BAD — takes 2 seconds total
const users = await fetchUsers();     // 1 second
const posts = await fetchPosts();     // 1 second

// GOOD — takes 1 second total
const [users, posts] = await Promise.all([
  fetchUsers(),
  fetchPosts()
]);

// PITFALL 2: await in a loop
// BAD — sequential, takes N * time
for (const id of ids) {
  const user = await fetchUser(id);  // One at a time!
}

// GOOD — parallel, takes 1 * time
const users = await Promise.all(ids.map(id => fetchUser(id)));

// PITFALL 3: Forgetting await
async function getTitle() {
  const res = fetch("/api/article"); // Missing await!
  const json = res.json();           // TypeError: res.json is not a function
  // res is a Promise, not a Response
}

Knowledge Check

1. What's the output order?

async function foo() {
  console.log("A");
  await Promise.resolve();
  console.log("B");
}
console.log("C");
foo();
console.log("D");
C, A, D, B. "C" runs synchronously. foo() is called: "A" runs synchronously inside foo. await suspends foo (everything after it becomes a microtask). "D" runs synchronously. Call stack is empty, microtask runs: "B".

2. What's the difference between Promise.all and Promise.allSettled?

Promise.all rejects immediately when any input promise rejects (fail-fast). Promise.allSettled always waits for all promises to complete and returns an array of result objects with status ("fulfilled" or "rejected") and value or reason. Use allSettled when you want all results regardless of individual failures.

3. Why is await in a for-loop usually a bug?

Each await pauses the loop until the previous iteration resolves. If you have 10 API calls that each take 100ms, a sequential loop takes 1000ms. Promise.all(items.map(...)) fires them all at once and takes ~100ms. Sequential only makes sense when each call depends on the previous result.

Exercise: Build a Retry-With-Backoff Utility

Create an async function retry(fn, maxRetries, baseDelay) that:

  • Calls fn() (which returns a Promise)
  • If it rejects, waits baseDelay * 2^attempt ms and retries
  • After maxRetries failures, throws the last error
  • Logs each retry attempt
const data = await retry(
  () => fetch("/flaky-api").then(r => r.json()),
  3,    // max retries
  1000  // base delay: 1s, 2s, 4s
);

Bonus: Add a shouldRetry predicate that checks the error to decide whether to retry.
